data.text:
INT: Part 2, tape 9, May 12, interview with Mark Verstandig. Last tape we were talking about what it means, or what it meant at that time, in the '30s in Poland, to be Jewish. We were talking about, or rather you were explaining about the whole process of registration and how people had to have their identities in place. Can you tell us more, please? 

MV: Yes. [INAUDIBLE] to this, were in order-- were-- were designed to keep a check on-- on people, on people's movements. Since it's for that reason, that when one, whether it was a Jew or whether it was an Ukrainian or a Pole, when he moved from one village to another, from one town to another, he had to fill out a form, stating his religion, his nationality, where he lives here, and the-- the destination of his travels, and the address where he is going to be, where-- where-- where he is going to reside in the town and village of his destination. 

My opinion, it was a part of keeping a check on people, of movement-- of-- of-- of the movement of people. Now as far as-- as far as nationality is concerned, people had to state nationality, their nationality, because the Versailles agreement or treaty, which established Poland, took into consideration that at least 75% of the Polish, of the population residing in the boundaries of Poland, belonged to minorities. And there were Jews, Germans, Ukrainians, and the others. 

In some places, let's say in western Galicia, in western Galicia, there were places where the majority of the population were Ukrainians. And north of-- of this part were Belarusians and-- and so on. But every, though is the-- is-- the minorities were, at that time, also interested that people should state their nationality. But the government, who did not-- did not try to force you to state your minority national-- your-- your-- your minority nationality. They would rather-- they would have rather been pleased if the Ukrainian will state that he is of Polish nationality. So that could be changed easily. 

Now, however, religion could not have been changed, because the parish has got the register of Catholics. And the-- the Jewish [NON-ENGLISH] the Jewish autonomic institutions, had the register of all Jews. If a Jew, as I stated, maybe, before you-- if a Jew was not circumcised, he could not be registered as a Jew. He could not be registered as a Jew in this register, which was-- which was held by an-- by a-- a clerk appointed-- appointed by the Jewish-- by the Jewish kehilla. I wouldn't say kehilla. The-- the-- Jewish-- here I would say a board of deputies, but it was not a board. It was a-- the-- the Jewish-- 

INT: Governing council. 

MV: Council. 

INT: Council of some sort. 

MV: Yes, it was the Jewish council. So he has to be registered there. And they would not accept him if he is not a-- if he is not circumcised. And the Catholics will not accept him because he is not converted. So he is-- he-- he-- he is as much as not born. He didn't exist. Therefore-- and-- and this state, this power of the religious minorities, of the religious minorities, was guaranteed in the Versailles-- in the Versailles treaty, together with the nationalities. But nationality you could change easily. 

Nobody will take-- nobody could-- could really force you to say today that you are of Polish nationality and tomorrow that you are a Jewish nationality or Ukrainian nationality. You could have-- you could change it any time, any time you wish. But as far as the religion is concerned, here you stayed. And the Jewish council had the right to impose taxes on you. And if you did not pay the taxes, the komornik, the-- the sheriff, an official sheriff of the court would take any-- any-- anything you owned, including the candlesticks, and auction them. 

They had the right to impose taxes. And you couldn't get out of it. Why? Because when you went out of Mielec to Cracow, you had to-- you had to fill out a form stating your religion and where you go. When you come to Cracow, you had to fill out a form that you arrived here, and you live here, and your nationality, and your religion again. 

Now, if you didn't-- if you were not taxed by the Jewish council in Mielec, you will be taxed by the Jewish council in Cracow. The only way out of it was conversion, no other way. And that was-- and for that purpose, the forms controlling the movement of the people were appreciated by the Jewish council just the same. Although, to a certain extent, when I look at it from the point of view of the-- of the situation, perhaps the situation of Australia or-- or in the Western world, I would say that, yes, that was one of the symptoms of a police state, keeping an eye on every person, on every individual in the country, on his movement. 

INT: Were these taxes that were imposed by the Jewish council-- 

MV: Yeah? 

INT: Were they instead of the government taxes or in addition to? 

MV: No, it's addition of-- it was in addition, and-- and their purpose was to keep the institutions of the-- of the Jewish community financed. They paid the rabbi. They paid the ritual slaughterer. They paid the-- the-- the Jewish judges, the so-called [NON-ENGLISH]. And they paid for the upkeep of the cemetery. And they have got-- they take-- they took care of all the charitable institutions, of all the needs, of all the needs of the Jewish people. 

To a certain extent, you can say that they-- that the-- that the Jewish council governed the Jew from the cradle to the grave, because the man who-- who performed the circumcision was paid by the government, by the Jewish council. And you went to the-- you-- you had to be buried in the Jewish cemetery, which belongs to the Jewish council. So, in this respect, one could easily say that they governed the Jew from the cradle to the grave. 

INT: Was this a situation for the majority of Jews in smaller places like Mielec, or also in the large cities? 

MV: In the large cities, it was even better organized. Was-- was everywhere, was everywhere, and that-- that was not only in-- not only in Poland, but in-- in almost everywhere. As a matter of fact, I think that in Sweden, up till now, the Jewish council has got the right to tax you for all those purposes. And they tax you in accordance with your-- in accordance with your income. 

I've got a cousin in Sweden, who told me that he is paid more than the-- the-- people, than the most-- than the majority shareholders of Volvo, because they-- because they somehow can-- can cover their real income. Whereas he was an employee. He was working for the government. So they-- they-- he couldn't-- he-- he couldn't do anything. They just taxed him in accordance with his-- with his real income. Now that-- that exists even in Sweden. 

INT: In this context that we're talking about, the idea of Jews being different nationals from Poles, was there, in your opinion, a-- a conception of people of Jewish origin as being not Polish, before the war? 

MV: Jews consider themselves Jews of national-- of Jewish nationality, living in Poland, being citizens of the Polish Republic. And the Polish Republic committed-- was committed to respect the nationality and the religion of all the minorities. It was on that condition that the-- that the boundaries of the Polish-- of the Polish state and the existence of the Polish-- of a Polish republic was established on the strength of the-- of the Versailles treaty. Because after all 35% were officially minorities. 

So you can't-- you-- you-- you-- you can't impose nationality of-- and a Polish nationality on a Ukrainian who lives in Lwow. And he-- he thinks that, really, Lwow shouldn't be part of Poland. Or in Vilna, who thinks that-- that Vilna shouldn't be part of Poland. But Vilna was part of the Poland on condition that the people living in Vilna could express themselves as Lithuanians, Lithuanians of-- in-- residing-- as Lithuanians of Polish citizenship. 

INT: So is it fair to say that, in the interwar years in Poland, the Poles of Catholic origin-- 

MV: Yeah? 

INT: --considered Jews as non-Poles? 

MV: They considered Jews as non-Poles. But that doesn't mean-- that doesn't mean that they couldn't be citizens of Poland. Because the Polish state was established in boundaries which comprised different minorities. They had all the-- they all were given citizenship of the Polish. But they had the right to keep their-- their Ukrainian, Jewish, German, Czech, and Lithuanian nationality. 

INT: So some of the other approaches towards Jewish nationality, such as English or German approaches before the war, where Jews were considered English or German, was a foreign concept in Poland. 

MV: That was a foreign concept in Poland. Because, first of all, in Germany, in Germany, there was a very small percentage of Jews. Let's say half a-- 0.5% or 0.4% of population were Jews. And other minorities were even smaller. Well, that's German was-- was a country, which was inhabited by Germans, where Poland was a new state comprising different minorities. And this state was a subject on the condition that the minorities will have the right to live as a minority with full citizenship. That is the difference. 

INT: Is it possible, in your-- 

[BACKGROUND NOISE] 

--is it possible in your mind to give a general overview of the relations between Polish Catholics and-- and the Jewish people between the years? 

MV: Yeah, you may be surprised when I tell you that the relations, as far as I could see them, were very good. I can tell you that a Jewish hawker, or a Jewish peddler, dressed like an orthodox Jew, in a black caftan, with the hat and with his sidelocks and beard, could walk through villages safer than, for instance, here in Australia, an elderly woman traveling in the train at night. 

The relationship between the Jews and non-Jews were very good. They knew. They took it from the church that the Jews are responsible for the killing of Christ. But they didn't take it in this minute that they-- that because of that they should kill the Jew of today. But the problem was that the Poles were, to a very large extent, illiterate, especially the people-- especially the people in the villages, the peasantry. They were illiterate. 

They were manipulated from time to time. They were manipulated by a small gang of agitators, of-- of Falangists. Whether they were-- they named themselves Falangists at that time or not, that is not important. But this is, is that they can come to town and to-- and to loot, and to-- but not to kill, but to loot. And that was what the-- what happened really when Poland was established in 1919. 

The trouble was that the antisemitic excesses sdfd always manipulated from outside by a small minority. That's one thing. And the next thing is that the-- that the government was behind it. Whether officially or not officially, it was behind it, because the government wanted sooner-- the sooner the better that the nationalities should disappear, that Ukrainians should become Poles, or that the Poles should Polandize the Ukrainian territories and that the Jews should become assimilated. 

The symbol of assimilation of a Jew was [NON-ENGLISH] from olden times, who organized, in the time of-- of Kosciuszko, who organized this regiment to fight the Russians and the Germans and so on. Now, that was the idea that the Jews should assimilate. At that time it was not a racial problem. It was a problem of assimilation, that the Jews should behave, give up their autonomy. And-- and-- and-- and even the better, the best solution would be if they-- if they-- if they accept the Catholic faith, not the Protestant, but the Catholic faith. That would be the best solution for-- that would be the best solution to solve the minority problem and the Jewish problem. 

That later changed. During the war, for instance, the underground papers of the Polish so-called underground, said that a Jew who accepts the Catholic faith became a Catholic, but he is still not a Pole. That was the official-- the-- the-- the-- the-- the-- the-- the official policy of the-- of the underground, of this antisemitic underground. 

So this is what I am telling you, that the situation, the relationship between the Poles and the Jews were not bad. The only trouble was when outside agitators incited-- incited the Poles. And the best-- and-- and the best evidence is that a Jewish peddler could walk tens of kilometers, walking because he didn't have a horse. He just-- he-- he-- he just walked. And keeping-- and keeping in a rucksack his merchandise, which he is selling, or keeping in a basket the [NON-ENGLISH] or the-- or the goose, which he bought in order to sell it in the town. And he went-- you could see him from afar, because he was wearing this black caftan and his wide hat, this black wide hat, with his beard and the-- nobody were stoning. Nobody-- nobody harmed him. 

INT: Was this still the case right up to the end of the '30s? 

MV: No. No. No. No. It was not until the end of the '30s. It was the middle of the '30s, when Adam Adam Koz came to power. The Falangist behind him, [NON-ENGLISH], behind him, were-- were organizing, were organizing already, were organizing gangs all over the place, and especially in-- in-- in-- in the bigger cities, more in the cities than in-- more in the cities than in the rural areas. 

INT: Talking about the rural areas, perhaps we can finish a picture that you were describing earlier about the socioeconomic life of the village, of the shtetl. 

MV: The shtetl and the village-- yes, I remember. We talked about it, and maybe I didn't elaborate. And I said that in the village they were eating meat twice a-- twice a year for Passover and-- for-- for Easter and Christmas. Whereas, in town, the town people had meat every day. There was-- I really should have qualified that, because as far as the rural population is concerned, I referred to the farmhand, to the poorest of the poor. And as far as a shtetl is concerned, I referred it to wealthy people. And there were wealthy people. 

There were landowners. For instance, in our-- in our Mielec, there were at least 10 families, landowners having large estates around. Now, beside-- a little bit lower, there where-- there were business people, merchants, business people, entrepreneurs. They-- they-- they had logging rights in large and vast forests, which belong to the aristocracy. 

They had sawmills, where the timber was processed. There were flour mills. There were-- there were-- there-- there were wealthy people. So, the wealthy people really enjoyed themselves, and they had meat. And they had all the luxuries in the world. 

But you come down to the last-- to the one before the last. Because the last one-- the last one would be the beggars. There were a vast amount of beggars, going from door to door begging. On a Sunday, they used to go to around the houses to beg for the remnants of the-- of the Shabbos, of the-- of the bread and of everything what was left over from Shabbat. Because you have to know one thing. The Jew, at that time, in the shtetl, whether he was rich or small or that, the Jew lived for the Shabbat. 

When it came Friday night, the world, the-- the world of the diaspora disappeared. He lived in his own world in the kingdom-- in the kingdom of Israel, in his own family. He dressed himself like one for-- for a-- for a special occasion, with his best clothes. He went to the synagogue, and he came back. And the Jews saved every penny-- we're talking about the lower classes-- in order to prepare for the Shabbat, to have wine for bless-- to bless the wine, to have fish, and to have a piece of meat, and to have white bread. It is not because it is in-- it is in order to dignify the Shabbat. The Shabbat, like Heine explains it in his poem, was Shabbat [NON-ENGLISH], is a-- the Prinzessin of Shabbat. The Jews lived for the Sabbath. 

And then we were talking about the lower class. There were masses of beggars, who really couldn't-- who really didn't have the-- that didn't have anything for breakfast unless-- unless they-- they-- they-- they could get some charity. And there were not enough charitable institutions to help them. There were not like we have got here, the-- the kitchens, [NON-ENGLISH], Salvation Army. It was nothing, nothing existed there. 

So these were the-- the lowest class were the beggars. And there were plenty of them. There were 10 times, 20 times, 30 times more beggars than on the top-- than the top rich people. But take, for instance, the workers. Take, for instance, the artists. I'll give you one example. 

When I was a student at the university-- when I was a student at the university, my family, my father and my mother, sent me every week a parcel. And the parcel was left-- a food parcel. The parcel was left with a Jew who was a porter. Porters in the town was one of the Jewish professions. Again, I remember where he lived, in a dark-- in a dark flat consisting of two dark rooms, two dark rooms. 

People used to leave parcels in the room-- in this room. His wife was there. And he used to take the parcels to Cracow. This man had a railway ticket, a half-yearly railway ticket. Again, we called him Rabbi [NON-ENGLISH] Birnbaum because he was dressed in a black caftan, beard and sidelocks. 

At 3 o'clock at night, he had a cab, a horse-driven cab, taking him to the train. He traveled from-- he traveled from Mielec to the [NON-ENGLISH], which took him an hour. There he had all these parcels around him, watching the parcels. He took the parcels down because he had to wait for another train. There was not a direct train into Cracow for an hour. Took another, later he-- he went to Cracow, another three hours. 

INT: Thank you. We'll have to continue a next tape.
